item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image is owned by Jeff Q. As there is no indication they have released the image under CC 3.0, the image is assumed not compliant and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this, made me feel bad for the doc-in-a-box

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Typo, grammar error?

Observer Note: Following expiration of D-3559, SCP-1930 appeared to have attempted to cut its own wrist with the scalpel before being forcibly withdrawn.

I assume the scalpel was withdrawn from the scip's reach, but it read like they somehow pulled a man out of a tiny box!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was neutral until it tried to cut its own wrist, at which point I went and upvoted, but by the end I'd removed it. I found the sounds entirely underwhelming and generic spooky, and didn't say anything that the wrist-slitting didn't. I think that part could be removed entirely and that would be much for the better. Didn't care for the attacking the fallen doctor, either. I thought it was extremely unlikely that the Foundation would use an ex-doctor as a test subject in a medical-related SCP without realising.

item.title: Re: Happy hundred!
item.content:encoded: Why the Gamzee Makara-style nose? :o)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a big fan of this one. It's so close to a hundred! Here's hoping some new blood sees this post and comes to give this SCiP attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd really like to see more done with this idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. The attempt at cutting itself was a nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 in memory of the doctor stuck in this thing. Knew I was going to upvote when I hit the suicide attempt after a failed operation, but his retaliation on the unethical surgeon really sealed the deal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, although I question the competence of researchers who would make use of a D-class subject while being unaware of what crime he was convicted for until after the experiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This hits all the right kinds of horrible. I almost want to write a short tale where the medikit was used near-constantly, maybe in a tria-—

I NOW KNOW WHAT MY GAME DAY PHASE THREE STORY IS *runs off flailing to a keyboard*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Archimedes, no! It's filsy in zere..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: good, I like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very intrigued here, but I feel like it could should be expanded upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe this explains those med kits in Left 4 Dead, etc. Could this just be a defective one?

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the attacking the fallen doctor, but I don't much care for the recorded sounds. Perhaps if they were the attacked doctor? That could be interesting, if this thing were a sort of purgatory for corrupt doctors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This raises some interesting questions. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And zis is how I lost my medical license!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was with you until the last two segments (the D-4819 with the skin rash and the digital recorder); those both struck me as trying way too hard to be scary, so they hit me as narm instead.

Clearly this isn't something that needs to be fixed, though, as everybody else seems to like it. Leaving a comment anyway because I figured that if I was going to be the only downvote I should say something to try to explain why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one just gets better and better and better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The execution just doesn't do justice to the intent, which is my main concern.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would work better, but it's a little too blunt. I get what you are trying to do, it's just not done well enough. And I also just like the oathbreaker angle a little more than the inept doctor.

What I mean:
Replacing one of the words "jerky" or "spasmodic" with 'shaky' can help connotate the idea of the object being nervous and unsure, rather then the current description which brings up the image of the nurses from Silent Hill.
I also don't understand why this object is indestructible, as I see no real reason why indestructibility is necessary to its function.
Naming the subject "Subject" also gets a little jarring to read without an article in front of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A doctor, "cursed" to live in a medkit, healing badly…? Not bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.
Upvoted.
The creep could be amped up a little, but upvoted nevertheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Generic spooky sounds?

Huh. I guess you can lead a horse to water, but you can't make it drink.

Try this instead: "Please let me out. I promise I won't screw up again. Please let me go."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very doubtful that the intent was to write a useful item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[REVISED FEEDBACK]

The test logs are rather same-y, with the exception of test #4. The idea that this ghost surgeon was trying to penalize a doctor for breaking the Hippocratic oath was very intriguing, but unfortunately this is never expanded upon. The discovery paragraph adds nothing to the article, and features redaction that just doesn't read well. The article also ends on a very bland note with audio logs which could be used to create something of interest, but instead feature generic "spooky" sounds that just don't gel well with any of the other ideas in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, VERY good job!
Had me wondering a bit at the start but shesh, what an excellent SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems pretty good, I like it! Upvoted.
